title: 2026/02/27 GA Subcommittee B [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-02-27 - Governance and Administration Committee - Emergency Management Bill [Subcommittee B, Part 2]
description: Friday 27 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee B Emergency Management Bill (No 2) Waste Management NZ Limited - Simon Pilkinton, Legal counsel - David Howie, Executive General Manager, South Dianne Downward Powerco - Jemma Gerring, General Manager Corporate Services - Carl Wallworth, Head of Risk - Mark Dunn, Head of Network Operations - Irene Clarke, Policy Manager Te Rūnanga o Ngāti Whātua - Ihapera Paniora - Maree Langdon Companion Animals NZ - Dr Nicola McDonald, Animal Welfare Manager - Pete Monk, CEO Civil Contractors New Zealand - Alan Pollard, Chief Executive Paul Brown
descriptionSummary: Emergency Management Bill. Emergency Management Bill (No 2). Waste Management NZ Limited

chunks.text: I will, uh, just give him a minute to turn on his camera.
Here we go.

Ah, Perfect. Fantastic. Welcome, uh, Simon and team from Waste Management. Um, you guys are up for the next 10 minutes, so I'll let you know when there's a minute or two to run. Um, but you might like to leave some time for questions. It's all yours. Um, thank you very much, um, to the, uh, um, so I'm Simon Pelkington.

I'm, um, legal counsel for Waste Management. I'm pairing here today with David Howie, who is the executive of General Manager of Waste Management. And I'll hand over to David, um, in a second. The submission from Waste Management, which which you'll have is, is, is quite a simple one really, but, but a very important one. Um, waste Management su, you know, supports the, um, intent of, of, of the bill and it, and, and it does.

So as New Zealand's largest, um, waste services provider, um, with, with operations, you know, right across the country, which, which are essential, um, are essential infrastructure. And, and, and essentially the, the submission, um, really is that that waste management needs to be included in schedule three of the bill.

Uh, it needs to be included so that it can be involved in, um, all of the, the, the planning, um, et cetera, that it's done, uh, in anticipation of, of these events rather than, um, rather than be left out and, and, and have to do its own thing, um, on an ad hoc basis, uh, when, when they occur.

Um, we are not, and, and we have looked, we are not really aware of the policy basis for the ex exclusion of waste services, um, as an essential infrastructure provider, not just from this legislation, but from other legislation. We, we, we just don't understand the, the policy basis for it.

There doesn't seem to be any, any basis for it. And so that is, that is the essential submission, um, from, from Waste Management to be included rather than have to go through the, um, order and council process, which will seem to be inevitable, um, really anyway. Uh, and so I'll hand over to, to Mr. Howie now, who, who, who, who, who can explain from Waste Management's perspective why, um, they think it, it, it is really critical to, um, to be included.

Thank you, Simon. And, uh, good morning, uh, to committee members. Uh, I think just to reiterate that we, uh, from a waste management perspective, uh, certainly support the, uh, the tender of the bill. And we've seen firsthand, uh, through a number of, uh, major events, uh, over the last, uh, 10 to 15 years, the importance and critical nature of being able to provide continuity and, and waste services, both the, the collection of materials from households and businesses and the safe, uh, consolidation and,

and ultimately disposal of that material, um, in times when there are, uh, major failings in, uh, other infrastructure, uh, as a result of those events. So I think my, my experience with Waste Management, uh, is in an executive role, uh, is, uh, has been for the last, uh, 12 years, um, and prior to that in the broader resource recovery sector, um, for, uh, sort of 20 years prior to that.

So I've seen, uh, a number of events, uh, sort of, that have had a major impact on the country, uh, both nationally and at a regional level. Um, and to just talk briefly about some of those, um, if I go back to the Christchurch earthquake, um, and the, uh, the events following that, uh, we as an organization, we are able to mobilize collection services to households, uh, the following day.

So the next morning, uh, we were able to get out, um, as you'd be aware, a complete failure of the, uh, wastewater services and, uh, much of the roading network, uh, made that very challenging for, uh, for residents. And, uh, for a lot of people, the only place they had to dispose of any materials was in their, uh, household wheelie bin.

Um, and, uh, to be able to safely and san, you know, sanitary collect that, uh, is, uh, was a, was a critical part of keeping, I guess public health and the city safe following that event, uh, and gave people, I think, confidence that, that some services were at least able to be up and running, um, at a regional, uh, regional level.

Uh, cyclone Gabriel, more recently in the Hawke's Bay, uh, the, the flooding and the damage that was caused there saw the local, uh, transfer station network for consolidation, uh, either underwater or inaccessible. And the local, uh, landfill for Hawke's Bay, uh, was cut off with roading access and, and damage to the site itself, which meant it was, uh, inaccessible Waste Management was able in conjunction with the local councils and the, um, uh, other operators in the sector to establish a interim transfer station at one of our sites.

Uh, and consolidate material both from residential and, and probably more importantly from a number of the commercial industrial providers that had had major freezer failures, um, and, uh, had a lot of cut traceable materials that were gonna create major health issues if they weren't, uh, safely disposed of, uh, and take that material away.

That the issue without being included in Schedule three under this legislation is that, that we would continue as we were in the Cyclone Gabriel example, to have to operate effectively outside of a consenting requirement, uh, to operate a site such as that. Um, so we just think we have business continuity planning.

We have a, a lot of work as, as an organization that we do to be able to provide immediate services, but to be able to integrate that with the emergency management teams, uh, across all of the other providers and be part of that from day one, uh, would we believe create a much better outcome for, uh, for communities and for the country in those emergency situations.

Um, COVID-19 obviously was a, was another example where, uh, the waste service was pulled in as a, as a, um, critical infrastructure provider later, but on a temporary basis. And again, uh, just being included in Schedule three would say as part of that on an ongoing basis.

So that's, yeah, I guess any, any questions probably is a good thing now. Yeah. We've got a question from Lamon, Lydia s Uh, thanks for your presentation. Uh, I just have a question just with regards to the criticalness of public health. So as an, as an operator and, and being left out is one thing, and so there's, uh, you raise a very valid point that the committee can discuss further, but in terms of actually keeping the wheels moving in terms of a recovery process, because we saw, uh, in, in terms of, uh, different parts

that disposal safely of rubbish is really important and people don't, are not fully aware people on the ground. So can you just explain a little bit further in terms of the criticalness of keeping the public health factors going in and after event? Sure. So, um, I think the, the Christchurch earthquake was a, was a really good example of that where, uh, where with wastewater services failing, uh, or failed, um, and, and to a certain extent in, in Hawke's Bay after cycling Gabriel, um, households, uh, didn't have access to, uh, toilet facilities, for example,

and they needed to dispose of that waste somewhere. Um, a lot of it found its way into oily bins, um, and uh, and we were able to collect that, um, and dispose of it, um, at a, at an appropriate facility that was able to cope with that material. Um, whereas, uh, um, yeah, so I, I think it's just being able to pick up those things, understand that the profile of the waste might be a little bit different to, to normal.

That, that, that people need to have the ability to dispose of materials safely. Um, and then to have the equipment available that can firstly collect that, but then get it to a site. Um, so a class one landfill that's got all of the appropriate environmental protections to be able to deal with that material safely, um, and get it away from, um, areas where it could impact public health.

Thank you. And, uh, we've just probably got half a minute to squeeze in. Andy Foster for one question. Uh, look, thank you and thanks for the work you do. Just very quick, um, question the, the difference between what you have to do now and what you would like to be able to do if you are a, um, a schedule three, uh, critical service, um, what difference does that make in terms of the ability to be able to get on and do the job and, and pick up these, um, uh, these hazard health hazardous wastes? Yeah, So I think I, I think the key difference

that we would see is, is being part of the planning and preparation prior to an event so that it can be mobilized more quickly and that the opportunity to be able to, uh, provide additional services, uh, that we perhaps can't provide without a, a time lag when they, uh, when, when an event occurs is what we would see as being the, I guess the, the key areas.

So it's being involved in the planning, being able to build those plans across different agencies and providers and have that, uh, I guess immediate response continuity available. So you would then get, I meet the immediate ability to be able to actually go out and do what you think you need to do in that situation. That's right, yes. Yeah. Okay, cool. Thanks. Thank you. I really appreciate the submission and, uh, really helpful, um, to understand that piece around lifeline.

So, um, thank you for your time. We are gonna move on to our next one. I believe Diane is online. Um, you can unmute yourself. Um, and, uh, there will be five minutes for this submission. And I'm also, just before you start, um, Limon and Lydia, Nia is gonna take over the chair, so she'll let you know when our time is almost up.

Okay, thanks. Yeah. Um, hopefully have time for questions. Um, so the main points of my feedback are practical ones in the hopes that the government will encourage and help people to prepare themselves for emergencies as, uh, I listed my written submission, but not limited to that list. So when the Alpine fault goes and it will rupture, all the planning and flow charts of power and responsibility will be good for starting a fire.

And nothing more power will be out for most people for weeks, months, and for some possibly years. The whole south island will look like Christchurch did on February 22nd, 2011. Some people on the flow chart will have perished. The A FA may have triggered the ang subduction zone to rupture and trigger volcanic eruptions and so on.

So Wellington and much of the north island could be suffering and will all be in the dark without means of communication if radio links or satellite comms aren't in place. This is the worst case scenario, but still a possibility. And planning needs to be for the worst, not for the best possibility. Therefore, local level planning, chain of command and stockpile of supplies, food, medical hand tools, et cetera, is imperative.

I'm not trying to fear monger, I'm just trying to be realistic here. The more people are prepared mentally, physically, emotionally, the more resilient they'll be. People who are trapped in their homes to do not be able to open their windows is a real concern and needs to be addressed. No one I've spoken to wants to take responsibility for this.

I've been told if the windows open fully, a child may fall out and hurt. But hey, how did my generation survive without all this health and safety over the top crap? Sorry. You may not think it is part of this bill, but if people die because they're trapped inside their homes or it makes it very difficult to rescue them from fire, flood, or earthquake because they can't get out, then it is part of this.

Bill. Another point about risk consultation. Nhu is a very successful multi-billion dollar corporation with a sizable real estate portfolio. Therefore, consulting with Nhu about areas of risk is a huge conflict of interest in my opinion. They may say that the management of their assets, such as the massive dairy farm by the river, is done through a third party, and therefore not a conflict of interest.

But you don't become so successful by not knowing the ins and outs of your own business involvements. And something else I didn't mention in my written submission is that insurance companies are beginning to dictate where people can live by demon, an area too risky. So Ralston, which is the fastest growing area in all of New Zealand, um, the insurance companies have stated that earthquake risk is too high and will no longer give insurance to new builds there.

Well, Ralston wasn't affected by the earthquakes we had, Christchurch was, but insurance companies will still ensure new builds here, even though we have slips, local drainage issues, flooding as well as earthquakes. When that was in civil defense, we were told that if the Y Mack River broke its banks, all of Christchurch would be affected. And that Christchurch should have been built at Ralston.

Therefore, you can consult all the geologists, all the community groups, farmers, and all the iwi about risk. But it's the insurance companies that will determine where people live and their judgment may be based on the stars more than actual risk to life and property. So I just wanna end with a couple in New Brighten, which is on the east coast of Christchurch.

We had a, a community meeting there and we were talking about risk and a Japanese Kiwi couple said they looked all around the world where, you know, with all the different risks involved where to live, they ended up in New brighten. 'cause they said, we choose tsunami. So I think people should have a right to choose where they live. So that's it.

Thank you Diane. Um, we've probably got enough time for a very quick question as any of our panel members, um, but we are out of time, but I really thank you for your submission today. Okay, well, just a practical founder's viewpoint, thank you very much for Thank you. Thank you. Uh, our next submitter is PGO Power Co.

Uh, we have Carl and Mark and Irene, uh, I believe they're with us. Yep, we've got Carl. Correct. Good morning everyone. We're just waiting for the others to attend. It's clicking through the, um, Okay. Okay. You've got 10 minutes, so over to you. Thank you.

Carl, did you wanna start? Yeah, I was just, I was gonna say, I was just waiting for my other colleagues to come on. I dunno if You can Gemma, you can hear Gem. Yes, I'm Gemma Gearing. I'm an executive manager at Powerco. I'm joined today by Mark Dunn, who's our head of network operations, re Clark, who's our policy manager, and Carl, who you've just seen Carl Woolworth, who's our head of risk.

Um, by way of brief background, Powerco owns the electricity and gas infrastructure that connects over 1 million Kiwis across the north island. We have critical responsibilities as a lifeline utility, and we operate across 29 different districts and six regions. We're also in the process of acquiring the first light electricity network, which covers the gisborne and areas.

We're acutely aware of the impacts and costs of emergency events, and in these events we see the variation and approaches across different regions. Carl's gonna speak briefly to some examples and planning and response, and then we'll leave time for your questions. Thank you, Carl. Okay, great. Thanks Gemma. And again, good morning everybody. Um, I guess there's two things really I'll talk to.

Um, so firstly, in terms of planning and information sharing. So in addition to our responsibilities, a as a lifeline utility, we voluntarily undertake climate related disclosures. Um, we publish those through our integrated report that's available on our website. Um, and it's actually something that we don't have to do, but we choose to do it. Um, what that has done is helped us actually form, um, climate adaptation and resilience plans for this.

What we do is we've assessed climate risks and our asset vulnerabilities across our network, and we've documented those risks and investment priorities. We then share this data on our assets and hazards. Um, and our plans going forward is to actually move to a more open platform on our network, similar to what we've currently got at the moment with our online capacity maps. We feel the bill could actually help in directing better sharing of data so that we can share this information two ways, particularly with those who we need to coordinate with, um, such

as other infrastructure owners across the country. Now, in terms of, I guess a real example for you, um, following Cyclone Gabrielle, a significant gas pipeline on our network, um, was impacted on a bridge crossing in Napier. Now that needed maintenance. Um, in terms of the fixings, it was damaged by, um, forestry slash coming down in the flooding.

The root of that pipeline was also across a road which also needed reinstatement work, but we were enabled to coordinate with the roading authority to undertake that work in a coordinated manner. Um, because we didn't have the right information, particularly around the works that that infrastructure provider was using, the priorities and the sequencing.

In terms of the restoration activities, we think that the regional plans could useful document, um, how those interactions should work and the sequencing and sharing of regional, um, lifeline utility data. However, national direction is needed to overcome some technical and organizational barriers. There's real opportunity we feel in terms of that significant step up of information sharing through the right direction in the bill.

And we've made some, um, recommendations in our submission, particularly on clauses 24 91 and 212. So that's the first point. The second one really is around emergency response. The storm, less than two weeks ago, saw 26,000 properties. Hard to believe that number without power on our network across, um, WUI, Rani, Manawa two, and the wire wrapper.

In a period of one week, a team of more than 250 field staff attended close to 1700 jobs, including more than 500 lines down. So yeah, incredible statistics. Interesting cycle in Gabrielle was around 1800 jobs. So you can see the scale of the work that was required. Um, communication with civil defense, um, councils and other life and organizations was critical in terms of how we prioritize the restoration.

So a great example would be there was an isolated community with medically dependent people, and we prioritized the restoration of power there thanks to communications with civil defense and that local knowledge. That being said, um, in situations, or sorry, in some such a cycle, and Gabrielle and other recent ones, the event, um, saw often multiple and duplicated and possibly even unjustified information requests that take our staff away from actually focusing on restoring the energy.

One example, um, we received an information request from a national agency that requested updates, um, on restoration and also any information around customer welfare concerns. Now that information had already been provided through the regional emergency response groups, and also we have our map on our website, which is, um, regularly updated.

So that information was there. The request actually required quite a lot of manual efforts, um, on our behalf, and took a member of the team more than three hours to respond to, um, which obviously prevented that member from working on the restoration efforts. And we actually didn't have any confidence that the information once it was provided was even used or actually was even required.

So again, our submission recommends that, um, there are amendments to adjust the roles, clarity of leads, and to avoid, um, the potential duplication. I just mentioned, again, recommendations on clause is a hundred twenty four fifteen and 212. So look, those were two, um, I guess key points that we wanted to raise with you.

If I kind of draw that to a, I guess a summary, you know, coordination is a specific purpose of the bill and we hope that the committee will take the opportunity, um, to make these improvements to our emergency management system. So I will pause there and we welcome any questions that you may have. Uh, thank you Carl, and to your colleagues, um, members, are there any questions, Andy, over to you.

Thank you. Um, and as a Powerco customer, I can say I'm very familiar with outages. We had a few of those. Um, just in terms of your, um, your transparency, um, you know, from a custom point of view, sometimes the transparency between your trade off, uh, between actually continually, um, doing work to fix a problem and uh, and actually doing any capital work to, um, avoid the problem in the first place, um, is absolutely, well, I'm not aware it's transparent.

Um, can you just sort of give us some highlights on that too? My, my question really is that transparency, but also your responsibility is to, um, to have a, uh, a reasonably robust network in the first place so that you actually have less outages, um, when, um, a storm event for example, occurs. Uh, yeah, I'm happy to take that one.

So we, you may be aware that we are price quality regulated by the commerce company. So we do publish annually in asset management plan, which outlines the capital work that we tend to undertake every year and the maintenance work that we have planned. Obviously when there's big storm events, um, we have to switch modes and it's not safe for us to carry on doing planned work and we divert resources.

Um, we are, we operate under a revenue cap. So the money that we spend repairing after storms is money that it comes off what we can spend on other work. Um, so there is a bit of a juggling act. Um, we do have, we are, we are measured, um, for how many outages we have and long, how long they go for.

Um, and there are, you'll probably be aware of penalties for breaching that. Um, however, we've found over the last few years, some of these massive weather events, there's nothing we could do to make our network stand up to those, particularly when we, um, you might be aware that there's consultation going on around changing the tree regulations.

Yes, on the biggest problems we have is when a, when a, you know, sort of massive tree comes down across our power lines, there's, there's nothing we can do to make them stand up to that. And it's all about the response. So it is a juggling act. I get that. Um, and if you, if you want to read our asset management plan, um, that's published every year, it should. So it's a great read. I can tell you that we try to give that transparency through that.

But, um, we're also doing a lot more on our, um, website trying to push out to the, uh, customers the work we're doing in each area so they can understand our investment plans. Dunno if you've have anything else to add, I shall Read, I shall read that with interest and possibly come back to you at some later stage. Very Good. Yeah. Hey, Jim, I, I could probably add as well that, um, I think one of the points I talked to very quickly was around, um, the climate adaptation. So we a lot, You probably got about 20 seconds

because we've gotta go into the next submission. But quickly, if you Can talk, I was gonna say reference them just in terms of we've done a lot of work around the vulnerability of our assets and how that filters into our asset management plan. And we're working with local, um, pilots across the lifeline groups to actually see how we can sequence that work better. Well, thank you very much, team for coming along.

It was really interesting to hear you. Thank you for your submission. Thanks. Great, thank you. Have a good day. Our next submission is we welcome, uh, RA and Marie over to you. Uh, there's five minutes for your submission. Thank you. Well done.

Natural Resources manager. And um, over here is my colleague Marie Langdon, our development manager. I just thought I'd just quickly speak to our, um, submissions, which I'm sure you've already had the opportunity to read. Um, so I didn't want to take too much time, but what I wanted to do was pass you over to our, um, to Marie to actually speak about the work that she does on a daily basis without any funding.

Um, ad hoc funding, ad hoc grants, um, running on the, the smell of an oily rag and the things that she's been able to achieve in the last two years. Behind us is our, so we cover from nui to, so from UA and KAHU in Northland down to, um, in ko.

So the crux of our submissions is really to try to, um, see what we do reflected in the, in the reform in the legislation, but to the extent that we are able to really plan for it on a yearly basis so that, um, when Marie goes through with you what she, um, has been doing, um, imagine the things that we could do if we were actually seen in the reforms.

Um, and we were really able to plan. So most of the submissions were submitted on the, in the last two years have been in opposition of most of the amendments. But I must say that this one is a really positive shift and we, um, really do, um, support the changes. We support it. Uh, and of course you'd see in the submissions that, uh, we, we want more than just engagement.

We want to have shared authority and we want to partner, and especially in terms of the responsibilities and obligations we have to maki, um, our people, um, in, we would love a seat at the table. You know, I understand that there are already provisions in the CDM groups and the CEG groups, however, we are not part of TE anymore.

So TE is trying to really, um, and so to have direct relationships with, with the Crown and, and ministers. So, um, we would like to have a, a formalized, um, seat at the table. Um, so we're, we're seeking a number of things in terms of the submissions around, you know, representation on welfare coordination groups, Northern Lifeline groups, um, and things of that nature because of what we've seen here with Cyclone Gabriel and the recent, um, weather events, um, and formalizing roles, uh, prior to, similar to some of your submitters that you've heard from this morning, um,

formalizing some roles prior to the emergency so that we are really able to be able to be better, better equipped and better to able to, uh, better able to assist the first responders. And we would love for some capability and capacity to support, um, the work that Marie does and, and within our wider hapu of course, and our marai, um, and, um, training, you know, allowing us to come along to the training that, um, first responders have.

And this is happening. We do have in two weeks time, sims training. Um, so that's really good. Um, climate and risk reduction support with that, um, and support with MARAI as emergency hub. So a lot of the, uh, the key themes of it is just, uh, a little bit more formalized support and a little bit more recognition, um, and opportunity for us to sit in those different spaces so that we are able to prepare.

And then one final thing, and I know that this doesn't sit within any formalized, um, legislation as we speak, but we are current, we've currently taken it upon ourselves to, um, start to develop and implement a, um, UA iwi wide emergency management plan, stepped in, um, tell Maori we have buy-in from all of our PSGE.
So,

so, um, we are moving through, um, what that framework looks like. Um, and then moving through to how we can have a unified coordination, unified communication, um, streams to really assist those first responders. So I'll hand it over to Marie. Thank you. Oh, sorry to, um, apologize. We're, um, you've probably got just about 30 seconds 'cause we need to go.

Oh, okay. I'll be real quick. I can speak talk. So, so I managed the unit, which was set up after Cycl and Gabriel. Um, so it's a unit that's dedicated to, uh, risk reduction and readiness first, first, first and foremost. So we've done a lot of work in the, since Cycl and Gabriel. Um, lots of education training, first aid, um, resourcing our marae.

We've upgrading, um, drinking water. We've done flood, um, protection, bund drainage. We've rebuilt, um, our nui so that they can receive the people we've upgraded our, so that they can receive their communities. We've, um, put on new elution blocks, um, we've done maintenance and maintenance programs.

We've got a RAI team that's out there, um, helping our and queer, um, we've resourced our warehouse here that's got all the generators and all the equipment. We've done that on the smell of it, oily rag. So I guess it would just be nice to know that, you know, we've shown that we're good partners and that the, you know, we can get some resourcing to help us to continue to do this.

You have 10 minutes for your submission and we have, I don't have a name. Is that, um, yourselves? Mc Yeah. Hello, I introduce myself. I'm I'm, I'm the CEO of Companion Animals New Zealand. Um, hi Andy, how are you? And um, Hey Pete. Nice see you.

Uh, it's a pleasure to be here to, um, just to run through a, a quick summary of, um, of our view on, uh, on the bill. Um, I'll hand over to my colleague, um, Dr. Nicola McDonald now to run through those things and be available for a few questions at the end. Alright, good morning. Um, I'm gonna share a presentation, uh, to support our submission.
Go ahead.

okay. Um, I see that
hopefully that's she correctly.

Can someone confirm that that's she correctly? So Yeah, be good Nick. Yeah. Alright. Um, thank you for the opportunity to speak. On behalf of companion Animals from Zealand today, uh, we'd first like to say that we, um, acknowledge and appreciate the progress made in the emergency management bill with the recognition of animals.

But there are still some critical gaps that we would like to point out that may limit effective emergency response in practice. Our goal is to make sure the bill aligns with existing animal welfare station and recognizes that people, animals, and environment are interconnected, which we refer to as a one welfare approach.

Companion Animals New Zealand is a national animal welfare charity. We manage the New Zealand Companion Animal Register, which holds more than 1.5 million animal records connected to microchip numbers and has over 800,000 guardian because we have more than a thousand approved users who can access our register.

And these include veterinary clinics, shelters, and animal control offices nationwide. And we run educational initiatives for animal guardians in New Zealand. And we're also a member of the NPI facilitated National Animal Welfare Emergency Management Sub-Function group. Our central message is that animal welfare and human safety are not separate issues in emergencies.

They are closely linked. Almost two thirds of New Zealand households have a pet, and these pets are considered family members by most guardians and provide significant emotional and social support in emergency context. This bond directly influences human behavior, decision making and recovery Planning for animals is therefore not an optional extra, it's part of effective emergency management.

There's strong evidence that people will risk their lives for animals during disasters, and of course, to leave the animals behind, they will take longer to recover from emergencies due to stress. And as an example, we witnessed very distressed forced out owners during cyclone Gabrielle trying desperately to locate or identify their animals.

We also know that people delay reparation if animals cannot come with them or some really into restricted areas to retrieve them. This has direct implications for public safety, responder safety, animal welfare and recovery outcomes. On the other hand, the families remain together and with the animals, uh, recovery and resilience and births,

while animals are referenced in the bill, we feel that some operational gaps remain animals are classified as property and they know this reflects legislation elsewhere, but would like it modified in this bill. The management of animals, um, in Clause 91 specifically is a bit vague. There are no clear powers to seize transport and shelter animals.

There are no, um, identification provisions for unidentified animals during the response. And there's currently no reimbursement framework for organizations that provide animal response capacity. We're proposing targeted amendments that are practical and proportionate. Recognize animal sentient, remove animals from property definition within the spill, include animals in the emergency definition, strengthen welfare standards and plans, set clear rescue and care powers, including temporary holding facilities for animals and allow systems for identifications

through microchipping and registration on the New Zealand Companion Animal Register and reimbursement provisions for charities and big clinics that are part of the volunteer response. New Zealand legislation already recognizes animals as sentient beings under the Animal Welfare Act. With the amendment from 2015, sentient means that animals are able to experience the world subjectively have emulsions and experience pain and pleasure.

We recommend that emergency management legislation reflect that same principle. This is not about elevating animals above people. Human safety must always come first. But we're asking for consistency across the legacy of the framework. We're asking that responders are given clear powers to actively cease transport, shelter, and provide care for animals wherever possible without having to make contact with the owner First.

Animals and emergencies may be tracked in buildings injured without food or water, or exposed to hazardous conditions. We're also asking for clarity around responsibility once those animals are in fear. Research and operational experience shows that separation from companion animals can have significant psychological impacts on people and may slow recovery.

In contrast, rapid reunification reduces distress, improves evacuation compliance, and reduces pressure on temporary shelters. We recommend enabling authorized tran personnel to microchip and register unidentified animals found during emergencies on the New Zealand Companion Animal Register. As this is a simple and effective tool to support reunification, there are also substantial costs associated with sheltering animals that are not reunited and remain long-term in shelter environments.

While prevention is the best preparation for these events when it comes to animal identification practices, there's still valid benefits in enacting a response when a disaster hits animal emergency response in New Zealand relies heavily on charities and veterinary teams. These organizations provide essential services during disasters, often at their own cost, and when they themselves have been affected by the emergency.

And the current model is not sustainable in prolonged or large scale events. We're recommending that reimbursement provisions be expanded to include pre-approved animal welfare organizations and veterinary clinics operating on the formal emergency activation with appropriate accountability and reporting for the event.

So in summary, we're asking for targeted amendments to align the bill with existing animal welfare and recognize animals as sentient beings to clarify responder authority, support, identification, and reunification, and enable sustainable response capacity.
